title: The Columbian Exchange
description: Test your knowledge of how contact with Europe affected the people, environment, and economies of the North American continent.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which group of foods was native to the Americas before 1492 and later spread to Europe?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Corn, chocolate, tomatoes, and potatoes
Spinach, cabbage, rice, and sugarcane
Oranges, olives, tea, and herring
Wheat, coffee, grapes, and bananas

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Columbian Exchange most change daily life in Europe after 1492?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about the crops that crossed the Atlantic—what new foods became everyday staples in Europe?

choices.content: American crops like corn and potatoes improved diets and fueled population growth.
European diseases spread quickly and wiped out large numbers of farmers.
New animals from the Americas transformed European farming and replaced local livestock.
Spices from Asia were shipped through American ports, which made them cheaper for Europeans.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Columbian Exchange most affect West Africa after 1492?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about what the Americas needed most in the Atlantic exchange system and how that need affected societies in West Africa.

choices.content: Millions of Africans were forced into the Atlantic slave trade, reshaping many communities.
New American crops replaced traditional African foods and erased local farming practices.
European livestock such as horses and cattle transformed African farming and transportation.
Silver from the Americas flowed into West Africa and created new trade networks.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did European colonizers value sugar and tobacco so highly?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about what Europeans did with sugar and tobacco after harvest—were they everyday foods, sacred items, or goods moved through trade for profit?

choices.content: They were profitable cash crops that were grown for sale in Atlantic markets.
They were prized as sacred medicines and religious items that Europe lacked.
They were used as gifts to make alliances and treaties with Native nations.
They replaced staple foods in Europe and ended widespread hunger.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the main purpose of plantations in the Americas before 1750?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about what made plantations profitable and why colonizers invested in them—were they for food security or for global markets?

choices.content: To grow cash crops, such as sugar, tobacco, and coffee, for export
To supply Europe with staple crops like wheat, corn, and potatoes
To produce cotton for Europe’s rapidly expanding textile industry
To provide family farms that supported self-sufficient colonial communities

assessment_items.question_content:
**How were plantations in the Americas and Caribbean able to produce crops on a large scale?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: How could plantations grow crops like sugar and tobacco so cheaply?

choices.content: They relied on the forced labor of enslaved Africans and Indigenous peoples.
They depended mainly on small family farms run by European settlers.
They attracted large numbers of European workers by offering high wages.
They used advanced machines to replace much of the human workforce.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did tobacco become such an important crop in the English colonies during the 1600s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about how planters made money in Virginia and Maryland.

choices.content: It was in high demand in Europe and brought great profits to colonial planters.
It required minimal labor to grow, so colonists could plant it while focusing on other work.
It was used as food for colonists and replaced wheat in the colonial diet.
It helped colonists make peace with Indigenous peoples who used it as currency.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the spread of smallpox affect the Americas after 1492?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about why Indigenous populations declined so sharply after Europeans arrived in the Americas.

choices.content: It devastated Indigenous populations, weakening Native societies and making conquest easier.
It affected European settlers in the Americas more severely than Indigenous peoples.
It spread mainly in Africa, reshaping trade networks and ending the slave trade in the Americas.
It helped Indigenous peoples by giving them immunity to other European diseases.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the main cause of high death rates among Huron and Iroquois peoples in the 1600s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about what Europeans unintentionally brought with them to the Americas.

choices.content: Epidemics of smallpox caused by European contact
Coordinated campaigns of violence by European colonists
A massive volcanic eruption in the Adirondack Mountains
A famine caused by poor harvests of corn and beans

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which disease is believed to have originated in the Americas and spread to Europe after 1492?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Most deadly diseases originated in Europe and traveled to the Americas. Which one is often argued to have gone in the opposite direction?

choices.content: Syphilis
Tuberculosis
Malaria
Smallpox

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best describes the 17th- and 18th-century economic system of mercantilism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colonies should serve the mother country by supplying raw materials and buying its goods.
Nations should allow free trade so merchants could extract wealth wherever they fould it.
Colonies should trade widely with many nations to strengthen global trade networks.
Nations should focus on growing food for local populations, instead of building overseas empires.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which drink, once enjoyed by Mesoamerican elites, became popular in Europe after 1492?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about the drink Europeans first tried in the Americas—made from cacao beans—that later became sweetened in Europe.

choices.content: Chocolate
Tea
Coffee
Wine
